refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve a measured p95 cycle time ≤ CT_target for the load→travel→dump cycle under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions, with cycle time performance monitored and reported via telemetry.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-002 is a KPI requirement that must be operationalized with measurable acceptance criteria (p95 percentile), explicit duty cycle definition, and telemetry-based verification. The refinement adds observability (telemetry reporting) to enable continuous performance validation and root-cause analysis of cycle time deviations.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect cycle time samples across ≥100 load→travel→dump cycles under defined site conditions; compute p95 percentile; compare against CT_target. Telemetry logs shall record cycle phase timestamps (load start, travel start, dump start, dump end) to enable phase-level performance attribution.
sub_frs: FR-CT-001: The NGHE solution shall measure and timestamp each phase of the load→travel→dump cycle (load, travel, dump) with ≤100 ms resolution.
FR-CT-002: The NGHE solution shall transmit cycle time telemetry (phase timestamps, cycle duration) to the monitoring system at ≥1 Hz frequency.
FR-CT-003: The NGHE solution shall execute load, travel, and dump operations with deterministic latency and minimal idle time to meet the p95 cycle time target.
FR-CT-004: The NGHE solution shall adapt cycle execution strategy (e.g., travel speed, dump duration) based on site conditions and duty cycle constraints without violating safety requirements.

candidate_dps: DP-CT-A: Implement a centralized cycle state machine in the Vehicle control module that orchestrates load, travel, and dump phases with hard real-time scheduling and phase-transition telemetry logging.

DP-CT-B: Distribute cycle phase control across Vehicle (motion control) and Teleoperation (operator command sequencing) with asynchronous event-driven coordination and telemetry aggregation at the NGHE Solution level.

DP-CT-C: Implement a hierarchical control architecture with a Vehicle-level cycle executor (deterministic phase timing) and a Teleoperation-level performance optimizer (adaptive speed/dump tuning) coordinated via a shared telemetry interface.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-CT-C: Implement a hierarchical control architecture with a Vehicle-level cycle executor (deterministic phase timing) and a Teleoperation-level performance optimizer (adaptive speed/dump tuning) coordinated via a shared telemetry interface.

potential_sources: Coupling between Vehicle cycle executor and Teleoperation optimizer via Telemetry interface (information coupling, not control coupling).
Coupling between Vehicle motion control (travel speed) and Teleoperation command sequencing (operator input timing) mitigated by explicit state machine handoff.
Coupling between Safety Services (safety constraints) and Teleoperation optimizer (speed/dump tuning) managed via SafetyAlert interface.
coupling_check.notes: DP-CT-C decouples deterministic cycle timing (Vehicle responsibility) from adaptive optimization (Teleoperation responsibility) via asynchronous telemetry. This reduces coupling compared to DP-CT-B (distributed control) and maintains flexibility compared to DP-CT-A (monolithic). Safety constraints are enforced at the Vehicle level and communicated to Teleoperation via SafetyAlert, preventing unsafe optimization.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle cycle executor can achieve ≤100 ms phase-transition latency with deterministic scheduling (real-time OS or firmware-level control).
Assumption: Teleoperation optimizer can compute adaptive speed/dump parameters within 1-2 cycle periods without destabilizing cycle performance.
Risk: Network latency between Vehicle and Teleoperation (via Network Link) may exceed 100 ms, causing stale telemetry and suboptimal optimization decisions.
Risk: Safety constraints (e.g., max travel speed, dump angle limits) may conflict with cycle time optimization, requiring trade-off analysis.
Risk: Operator input timing (manual sequencing) may introduce unpredictable delays in phase transitions, limiting p95 cycle time predictability.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the degree of automation vs. operator control in cycle sequencing. If fully autonomous (no operator input per phase), DP-CT-C is robust. If operator-driven, add a sub-FR for operator response time budgeting and consider DP-CT-A (centralized, deterministic) as a fallback for high-criticality sites.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns the cycle state machine, phase-transition timing, motion control (travel speed, dump actuation), and real-time telemetry generation for load→travel→dump cycle execution. It enforces safety constraints (speed limits, angle limits) and reports cycle phase timestamps and duration via the Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits cycle phase timestamps (load_start, travel_start, dump_start, dump_end), cycle duration, and phase-level performance metrics (e.g., travel distance, dump angle) at ≥1 Hz to enable monitoring and optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives cycle sequencing commands (load, travel, dump) and adaptive parameters (target travel speed, dump duration) from Teleoperation or autonomous planner.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives safety constraints and alerts (e.g., max speed, geofence violations, emergency stop) from Safety Services and enforces them in cycle execution.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator and would require cycle execution logic to be distributed across multiple sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). High diffusion risk; not recommended as primary IM boundary for cycle execution.

Vehicle is the natural owner of cycle state machine, motion control, and real-time phase timing. All cycle execution logic is cohesive within Vehicle. Telemetry output and Tasking/SafetyAlert inputs are well-defined external interfaces. Minimal diffusion risk.

Teleoperation is responsible for operator command sequencing and adaptive optimization, not deterministic cycle execution. Assigning cycle state machine to Teleoperation would couple operator input timing to cycle timing, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Safety Services enforces safety constraints but does not own cycle execution logic. Assigning cycle timing to Safety Services would conflate safety monitoring with operational control. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module contains the cycle state machine, phase-transition logic, motion control, and telemetry generation. Teleoperation and Safety Services interact with Vehicle via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry), enabling decoupled optimization and safety enforcement without diffusing cycle execution logic.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle cycle executor is implemented as firmware in a legacy ECU with limited real-time capabilities, achieving ≤100 ms phase-transition latency may be infeasible, requiring architectural revision.
Risk: If Teleoperation optimizer requires sub-100 ms feedback loops to adapt cycle parameters, network latency on Network Link may exceed the control loop bandwidth, causing instability.

Risk: If Safety Services constraints are added dynamically (e.g., geofence updates), Vehicle must handle constraint updates without interrupting cycle execution, requiring robust constraint-handling logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Validate that Vehicle firmware/OS can support hard real-time cycle state machine execution with ≤100 ms phase-transition latency. If not, consider a hybrid approach: Vehicle executes deterministic phase timing; a real-time co-processor or edge controller handles adaptive optimization. Clarify the frequency and latency of SafetyAlert updates to ensure Vehicle can respond within cycle timing constraints.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-CT-C (hierarchical control with Vehicle cycle executor and Teleoperation optimizer) directly addresses the refined FR by separating deterministic cycle timing (Vehicle) from adaptive optimization (Teleoperation). The DP enables measurable cycle time performance via telemetry and supports safety constraint enforcement. No logical gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Telemetry interface must be implemented consistently across Vehicle and Teleoperation to avoid data format mismatches. Recommend defining a shared telemetry schema (e.g., protobuf, JSON) at the NGHE Solution level.

Moderate risk: Tasking interface must support both autonomous and operator-driven cycle sequencing. If operator input timing is unpredictable, cycle time p95 may be dominated by operator latency, not Vehicle execution. Recommend clarifying automation level.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module contains cycle execution logic with high cohesion (state machine, motion control, telemetry generation). External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimal. No significant diffusion of cycle execution logic across modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Cycle time measurement is feasible via Vehicle telemetry (phase timestamps). p95 percentile computation requires ≥100 cycle samples, which is achievable in a typical duty cycle test. Phase-level attribution (load, travel, dump) enables root-cause analysis of cycle time deviations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-CT-001 (phase measurement) is allocated to Vehicle telemetry generation. FR-CT-002 (telemetry transmission) is allocated to Vehicle and Network Link. FR-CT-003 (deterministic execution) is allocated to Vehicle cycle executor. FR-CT-004 (adaptive strategy) is allocated to Teleoperation optimizer. Clear allocation with no gaps.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the automation level: Is cycle sequencing fully autonomous (Vehicle state machine drives phase transitions) or operator-driven (Teleoperation sends phase commands)? This affects cycle time predictability and p95 achievability.
Define the Telemetry interface schema (phase timestamps, cycle duration, performance metrics) at the NGHE Solution level to ensure consistency across Vehicle and Teleoperation.
Specify the maximum acceptable latency for SafetyAlert updates and Tasking commands to ensure Vehicle can respond within cycle timing constraints.
Validate Vehicle firmware/OS real-time capabilities to confirm ≤100 ms phase-transition latency is achievable.

decision.justification: The refined FR and selected DP are logically sound and well-allocated to the Vehicle IM boundary. However, the sub-FRs (FR-CT-001 through FR-CT-004) require further decomposition to address implementation details, interface specifications, and real-time constraints. Specifically, FR-CT-003 (deterministic execution) and FR-CT-004 (adaptive strategy) need detailed DPs and IMs to resolve the coupling between Vehicle cycle executor and Teleoperation optimizer, and to validate real-time feasibility.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-CT-001: The NGHE solution shall measure and timestamp each phase of the load→travel→dump cycle (load, travel, dump) with ≤100 ms resolution.
FR-CT-002: The NGHE solution shall transmit cycle time telemetry (phase timestamps, cycle duration) to the monitoring system at ≥1 Hz frequency.
FR-CT-003: The NGHE solution shall execute load, travel, and dump operations with deterministic latency and minimal idle time to meet the p95 cycle time target.
FR-CT-004: The NGHE solution shall adapt cycle execution strategy (e.g., travel speed, dump duration) based on site conditions and duty cycle constraints without violating safety requirements.

assumptions: Vehicle firmware/OS supports hard real-time cycle state machine execution with ≤100 ms phase-transition latency.
Cycle sequencing is fully autonomous (Vehicle state machine drives phase transitions) or operator input latency is budgeted separately.
Network Link latency is <100 ms to enable timely telemetry feedback for Teleoperation optimization.
Safety constraints (speed limits, angle limits) are static or updated at low frequency (≤1 Hz) to avoid disrupting cycle execution.
Site conditions (terrain, load mass, dump angle) are known or estimated at the start of each cycle, enabling Teleoperation to compute adaptive parameters.
open_risks: Risk: Operator input timing may introduce unpredictable delays in phase transitions, limiting p95 cycle time predictability. Mitigation: Clarify automation level and budget operator response time.

Risk: Network latency between Vehicle and Teleoperation may exceed 100 ms, causing stale telemetry and suboptimal optimization. Mitigation: Validate Network Link latency and consider edge-based optimization if needed.
Risk: Safety constraints may conflict with cycle time optimization, requiring trade-off analysis. Mitigation: Define safety-performance trade-off curves and validate with Safety Manager.
Risk: Vehicle firmware may not support hard real-time cycle execution. Mitigation: Validate real-time capabilities early in design; consider co-processor or edge controller if needed.
evidence_needed: Vehicle firmware/OS real-time capability assessment (latency, jitter, scheduling guarantees).
Network Link latency measurement under typical site conditions.
Operator response time data (if operator-driven sequencing) or autonomous cycle sequencing specification.
Safety constraint definitions (speed limits, angle limits, geofence rules) and conflict analysis with cycle time optimization.
Duty cycle specification (load mass, travel distance, dump angle, site terrain) for cycle time target validation.
